title: SCRUM Fundamentals Mock Exam
subtitle: 40 Questions with SBOK Explanations
description: Complete 40-question Scrum Fundamentals mock exam based on the provided SBOK-aligned source.

questions.q: Who is responsible for maximizing the business value of the product?
questions.explain: The Product Owner represents the customer and stakeholders and is responsible for maximizing business value by prioritizing the Product Backlog.
questions.deep: In Scrum, value optimization is centralized in the Product Owner role so one person remains accountable for ordering work against business outcomes.

questions.q: What is the time-boxed iteration in Scrum called?
questions.explain: A Sprint is a time-boxed iteration during which the Scrum Team creates a potentially shippable product increment.
questions.deep: The Sprint is the core delivery cadence in Scrum. It creates rhythm, limits delay, and provides regular opportunities for inspection and adaptation.

questions.q: Which role is responsible for removing impediments for the Scrum Team?
questions.explain: The Scrum Master ensures the team follows Scrum practices and removes impediments affecting productivity.
questions.deep: Removing impediments protects team flow. It is part of the Scrum Master's servant-leadership role rather than line-management authority.

questions.q: Which artifact contains a prioritized list of product requirements?
questions.explain: The Product Backlog is a prioritized list of business and project requirements maintained by the Product Owner.
questions.deep: The backlog is intentionally dynamic. It evolves as the team learns more about customer needs, constraints, and delivery progress.

questions.q: Which Scrum event occurs every day?
questions.explain: The Daily Scrum is a 15-minute meeting where the team synchronizes activities and plans work for the next 24 hours.
questions.deep: Its value is short-cycle coordination. The Daily Scrum keeps the Sprint plan alive instead of leaving it static after planning.

questions.q: Who owns the Sprint Backlog?
questions.explain: The Scrum Team owns and manages the Sprint Backlog, which contains tasks required to complete selected Product Backlog items.
questions.deep: This ownership reinforces self-organization. The team commits to Sprint work and manages the execution details needed to complete it.

questions.q: What is the recommended size of a Scrum Team?
questions.explain: SBOK recommends Scrum Teams of six members plus or minus three, meaning 3-9 members, to maintain effective communication.
questions.deep: That range balances coordination cost and delivery capability. Teams that are too large lose communication efficiency, while teams that are too small may lack needed skills.

questions.q: What is produced at the end of each Sprint?
questions.explain: Each Sprint results in a potentially shippable product increment, which is the sum of completed Product Backlog items.
questions.deep: A Scrum Sprint measures progress through usable output, not just effort spent. The increment is the clearest evidence of real progress.

questions.q: Which event demonstrates completed work to stakeholders?
questions.explain: The Sprint Review is used to present the product increment to stakeholders and gather feedback.
questions.deep: The Sprint Review turns delivery into learning. Demonstration is useful because it invites real feedback on real work.

questions.q: Who decides how the work will be performed?
questions.explain: Scrum Teams are self-organizing, meaning they determine how work will be completed.
questions.deep: Scrum separates business direction from implementation control. The Product Owner decides what matters most, while the team decides how to deliver it.

questions.q: What is the purpose of Sprint Planning?
questions.explain: Sprint Planning defines the Sprint Goal and selects Product Backlog items that the team commits to completing.
questions.deep: A strong Sprint Plan aligns purpose and scope. It is not just task selection, but a shared intent for the Sprint.

questions.q: What is the main purpose of the Sprint Retrospective?
questions.explain: The Retrospective enables the team to inspect the previous Sprint and identify improvements.
questions.deep: Scrum improves both product and process. Retrospectives keep the team from repeating the same delivery problems Sprint after Sprint.

questions.q: What does Scrum emphasize for managing complex projects?
questions.explain: Scrum is based on empirical process control, which relies on transparency, inspection, and adaptation.
questions.deep: Complex work cannot be fully understood in advance. Scrum uses short feedback cycles to learn from reality rather than over-relying on prediction.

questions.q: Which Scrum artifact tracks work remaining in a Sprint?
questions.explain: A Burndown Chart shows remaining work versus time during a Sprint.
questions.deep: The Burndown is useful because it quickly shows whether the Sprint trajectory is improving, stalling, or slipping.

questions.q: Which Scrum value emphasizes honest communication?
questions.explain: Openness is a Scrum value that encourages transparency and clear communication within the team.
questions.deep: Without openness, inspection loses accuracy. Scrum depends on visible progress, visible problems, and visible tradeoffs.

questions.q: Who prioritizes Product Backlog items?
questions.explain: The Product Owner ensures backlog items are prioritized according to business value.
questions.deep: Prioritization is a value-management decision. Scrum keeps that accountability with the Product Owner rather than dispersing it across the team.

questions.q: Which meeting is limited to 15 minutes?
questions.explain: The Daily Scrum is a 15-minute time-boxed meeting.
questions.deep: The short duration keeps it focused on coordination and immediate planning rather than deep discussion.

questions.q: Scrum teams should be:
questions.explain: Scrum emphasizes self-organization, allowing teams to determine the best way to accomplish work.
questions.deep: Self-organization improves responsiveness because the people closest to the work make the execution decisions.

questions.q: What is the Definition of Done?
questions.explain: The Definition of Done is a shared understanding of what it means for work to be complete.
questions.deep: It protects quality and consistency by preventing the team from applying different completion standards to similar work.

questions.q: What does Scrum encourage for project delivery?
questions.explain: Scrum promotes iterative and incremental delivery to provide value early and frequently.
questions.deep: Incremental delivery lowers risk and creates earlier opportunities to validate whether the product is moving in the right direction.

questions.q: Who represents stakeholder interests?
questions.explain: The Product Owner represents customers and stakeholders.
questions.deep: This role translates stakeholder interests into product priorities and makes tradeoff decisions visible in the backlog order.

questions.q: What does a Sprint Backlog contain?
questions.explain: The Sprint Backlog contains selected backlog items and the implementation tasks for the current Sprint.
questions.deep: It is not a product-wide planning artifact. It is the team's focused operational plan for the active Sprint.

questions.q: What principle allows Scrum teams to adapt quickly?
questions.explain: Empirical process control supports frequent adaptation through inspection and adaptation.
questions.deep: Scrum works because teams inspect real outcomes and then adjust, rather than waiting until late phases to discover problems.

questions.q: Scrum promotes what type of development?
questions.explain: Scrum work is delivered in short cycles and grows the product iteratively and incrementally.
questions.deep: This approach creates continuous feedback, smaller risk windows, and better control over changing requirements.

questions.q: What is the main goal of Scrum?
questions.explain: Scrum focuses on maximizing value through frequent delivery.
questions.deep: The framework is designed to move useful outcomes into users' hands early, not to optimize documentation or upfront certainty.

questions.q: Who attends the Sprint Review?
opts: Only the Product Owner and Scrum Master
questions.explain: The Review includes stakeholders so they can see the increment and provide feedback.
questions.deep: That stakeholder interaction is what turns the Sprint Review into a product-learning event rather than just an internal status update.

questions.q: Which artifact evolves throughout the project?
questions.explain: The Product Backlog is continuously refined and reprioritized.
questions.deep: Because knowledge changes over time, Scrum expects the backlog to remain flexible instead of fixed.

questions.q: What is velocity?
opts: Amount of work completed in a Sprint
Number of blockers raised each day
Average duration of a Daily Scrum
Time needed to release to production
questions.explain: Velocity measures team productivity across Sprints by tracking completed work.
questions.deep: Its main use is forecasting. Treated correctly, it helps planning; treated badly, it becomes a misleading target.

questions.q: What type of teams does Scrum require?
questions.explain: Scrum Teams must have all skills needed to deliver increments.
questions.deep: Cross-functionality reduces handoffs and dependency delays, which is essential for short Sprint cycles.

questions.q: What happens to unfinished work?
opts: Counted as done if mostly complete
questions.explain: Unfinished items are returned to the Product Backlog and reprioritized.
questions.deep: Scrum avoids hiding incomplete work. Sending it back to the backlog keeps prioritization honest.

questions.q: What is the purpose of backlog refinement?
questions.explain: Refinement improves backlog readiness by clarifying, splitting, and estimating items.
questions.deep: Well-refined items reduce confusion during Sprint Planning and improve commitment quality.

questions.q: Scrum encourages which communication style?
questions.explain: SBOK highlights direct communication for efficiency.
questions.deep: Direct communication shortens feedback loops and reduces the distortion that accumulates through layers of reporting.

questions.q: Who ensures Scrum practices are followed?
questions.explain: The Scrum Master acts as facilitator and coach for Scrum practices.
questions.deep: The role helps the team use Scrum effectively without turning the framework into command-and-control process policing.

questions.q: What does transparency ensure?
questions.explain: Transparency is essential for inspection because it creates a shared understanding of progress.
questions.deep: If the current state of work is not visible and trusted, adaptation decisions will be based on false assumptions.

questions.q: Which meeting occurs after Sprint Review?
questions.explain: The Sprint Retrospective follows the Sprint Review and focuses on process improvement.
questions.deep: The sequence matters: first inspect the product with stakeholders, then inspect the team's way of working.

questions.q: What is the key output of the Sprint Review?
questions.explain: Feedback from stakeholders helps refine backlog priorities.
questions.deep: Scrum gains much of its adaptability from this loop between delivered increment and stakeholder response.

questions.q: What is a key advantage of Scrum?
questions.explain: Iterative cycles allow quick response to changing requirements.
questions.deep: Scrum does not remove uncertainty. It makes uncertainty manageable by shortening the decision-and-feedback cycle.

questions.q: What ensures product quality in Scrum?
questions.explain: The Definition of Done defines criteria for completion and supports quality.
questions.deep: Quality in Scrum is enforced through shared standards, not by assuming all completed work is equally complete.

questions.q: What is time-boxing?
opts: Tracking time spent by each individual
questions.explain: Scrum events have fixed maximum durations, which is what time-boxing means.
questions.deep: Time-boxing constrains cost, creates urgency, and prevents analysis or discussion from expanding without limit.

questions.q: What is the core Scrum philosophy?
opts: Lock all requirements before work begins
Delay delivery until everything is finished
questions.explain: Scrum focuses on incremental value delivery with continuous feedback.
questions.deep: Its philosophy is practical rather than predictive: build, inspect, learn, and improve continuously.
